tossups.question: The arietta “Caro mio ben” is attributed to any of three composers whose surname matches this regex, a father and two brothers, the younger of whom is known by its diminutive. After two refusals, Victorien Sardou let a then- obscure composer whose surname matches this regex adapt his play into a verismo opera in which the nihilist Loris Ipanov, premiered by Enrico Caruso, is accused of murdering Vladimir by the title princess, Fedora. Before he was a Wagner specialist, tenor Giuseppe Borgatti created the title role of an opera with an Illica libretto by a composer with this surname, in which the jealous revolutionary Gérard is finally repulsed after a heartbreaking aria that begins “they killed my mother” and ends “take my body, I’m already dead” sung by Madeleine de Coigny, who bribes a jailer to be guillotined with her poet lover. For 10 points, the composer of “La mamma morta” from Andrea Chénier has what surname?
tossups.question_sanitized: The arietta "Caro mio ben" is attributed to any of three composers whose surname matches this regex, a father and two brothers, the younger of whom is known by its diminutive. After two refusals, Victorien Sardou let a then- obscure composer whose surname matches this regex adapt his play into a verismo opera in which the nihilist Loris Ipanov, premiered by Enrico Caruso, is accused of murdering Vladimir by the title princess, Fedora. Before he was a Wagner specialist, tenor Giuseppe Borgatti created the title role of an opera with an Illica libretto by a composer with this surname, in which the jealous revolutionary Gerard is finally repulsed after a heartbreaking aria that begins "they killed my mother" and ends "take my body, I'm already dead" sung by Madeleine de Coigny, who bribes a jailer to be guillotined with her poet lover. For 10 points, the composer of "La mamma morta" from Andrea Chenier has what surname?
tossups.answer_sanitized: /Giordan[io]/ [Giuseppe Giordani, Tommaso Giordani, Carmine Giordani; Umberto Giordano, Giordanello]

tossups.question: One character in this play rebukingly asks another if he "thinke to roote [him] selfe in dead mens graves" like a yew tree, echoing a dream another character relates in which the branch of a yew kills two people trying to uproot it. Another character in this play, Marcello, reminds his mother of his infant brother breaking a crucifix just before that brother stabs him in the back. In a parody of the rite of extreme unction, two characters dressed as Capuchin monks mock a dying man before strangling him. Monticelso, fresh from his election as Pope, is drawn into a conspiracy to avenge his nephew's murder by Lodovico, who couches the information as a confession. A Conjurer shows this play's ostensible title character a pair of visions in the form of dumbshows in Act II, Scene 2 of this play, which depict the murder of Camillo by Flamineo and the poisoning of Isabella. For 10 points, relating the murderous and adulterous affair of the Duke of Bracciano and Vittoria Corombona, this is what revenge tragedy by John Webster?
tossups.answer: The White Devil, or the Tragedy of Paolo Giordano Ursini, the Duke of Brachiano, with the Life and Death of Vittoria Corombona, the Famous Venetian Curtizan [accept Vittoria Corombona before she is named]
tossups.answer_sanitized: The White Devil, or the Tragedy of Paolo Giordano Ursini, the Duke of Brachiano, with the Life and Death of Vittoria Corombona, the Famous Venetian Curtizan [accept Vittoria Corombona before she is named]
tossups.question_sanitized: One character in this play rebukingly asks another if he "thinke to roote [him] selfe in dead mens graves" like a yew tree, echoing a dream another character relates in which the branch of a yew kills two people trying to uproot it. tossups.question: In From Heaven to Arcadia, Ingrid Dowland suggests that this philosopher’s only play, a comedy called The Candlemaker, inspired the tone of Shakespeare’s Hamlet and other plays. One of this philosopher’s treatises largely consists of a series of “dramatic sonnets” between the poet Luigi Tansillo and a naval officer named Cicada on the purity of “philosophical love.” This philosopher quotes the poetry of Philip Sidney in his treatise on the “madness of love,” The Heroic Frenzies, which is one of a set of six dialogues this man wrote, including The Expulsion of the Triumphant Beast and The Ash Wednesday Supper. This philosopher was inspired by Hermetic doctrines to reject Aristotelian accounts of motion in his book Cause, Principle and Unity, which also provided a pantheist account of the universe. The thought of Nicholas of Cusa inspired this thinker to put forth a view that the world is infinite and that the Sun is at the center of the Solar System. For 10 points, name this philosopher from Nola who was burned at the stake partly for his Copernican world view.
tossups.question_sanitized: In From Heaven to Arcadia, Ingrid Dowland suggests that this philosopher's only play, a comedy called The Candlemaker, inspired the tone of Shakespeare's Hamlet and other plays. One of this philosopher's treatises largely consists of a series of "dramatic sonnets" between the poet Luigi Tansillo and a naval officer named Cicada on the purity of "philosophical love." This philosopher quotes the poetry of Philip Sidney in his treatise on the "madness of love," The Heroic Frenzies, which is one of a set of six dialogues this man wrote, including The Expulsion of the Triumphant Beast and The Ash Wednesday Supper. This philosopher was inspired by Hermetic doctrines to reject Aristotelian accounts of motion in his book Cause, Principle and Unity, which also provided a pantheist account of the universe. The thought of Nicholas of Cusa inspired this thinker to put forth a view that the world is infinite and that the Sun is at the center of the Solar System. For 10 points, name this philosopher from Nola who was burned at the stake partly for his Copernican world view.

tossups.question: This composer wrote an opera whose title character reminds Napoleon that he owes her sixty francs for laundry, Madame Sans-Gêne [sawn-jen]. Elena Obraztsova, Rosalind Plowright, and Fedora Barbieri are among the singers who have made cameo appearances in productions of this composer’s most famous opera, playing old blind Madelon. This man’s most famous opera opens with an apostrophe to a blue sofa, while another aria from that opera is sometimes called the “Improvviso,” instead of its first line “Un dì all’azzurro spazio,” because it follows the claim that true poetry cannot be produced on command. Enrico Caruso shot to fame after his performance as Loris, who sings “Amor ti vieta” in this composer’s opera (*) Fedora. In this composer’s best-known aria, the singer remembers Love telling her to keep on living after her house was burned and her mother killed. That aria, “La mamma morta,” is sung by Maddalena to Gérard, who retracts his accusations against her lover during the Reign of Terror. For 10 points, name this proponent of verismo who composed Andrea Chénier.

packet.name: 05 - PROBABLY BE SKEPTICAL OF THA POLICE

tossups.question_sanitized: This composer wrote an opera whose title character reminds Napoleon that he owes her sixty francs for laundry, Madame Sans-Gene [sawn-jen]. Elena Obraztsova, Rosalind Plowright, and Fedora Barbieri are among the singers who have made cameo appearances in productions of this composer's most famous opera, playing old blind Madelon. This man's most famous opera opens with an apostrophe to a blue sofa, while another aria from that opera is sometimes called the "Improvviso," instead of its first line "Un di all'azzurro spazio," because it follows the claim that true poetry cannot be produced on command. Enrico Caruso shot to fame after his performance as Loris, who sings "Amor ti vieta" in this composer's opera (*) Fedora. In this composer's best-known aria, the singer remembers Love telling her to keep on living after her house was burned and her mother killed. That aria, "La mamma morta," is sung by Maddalena to Gerard, who retracts his accusations against her lover during the Reign of Terror. For 10 points, name this proponent of verismo who composed Andrea Chenier.

bonuses.leadin: Caspar Shoppe wrote a scathing eyewitness account of this man’s execution, noting: “thus he miserably burned, and now probably dwells [in] those other Worlds he invented.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this man, who was executed in 1600 after returning to Italy from exile to teach Giovanni Mocenigo memory techniques. He argued for a heliocentric universe that was infinite.

This Cardinal was the lead prosecutor at Bruno’s trial, performing a similar role in the later trial of Galileo. This man wrote numerous papers attacking the oath of allegiance required of English Catholics by James I.
Bruno’s teachings also got him excommunicated from a religious group named for this German priest whose 95 Theses kickstarted the Protestant Revolution.
answers_sanitized: Martin Luther [accept Lutherans; accept the Lutheran Church]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Caspar Shoppe wrote a scathing eyewitness account of this man's execution, noting: "thus he miserably burned, and now probably dwells [in] those other Worlds he invented." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this man, who was executed in 1600 after returning to Italy from exile to teach Giovanni Mocenigo memory techniques. He argued for a heliocentric universe that was infinite.

This Cardinal was the lead prosecutor at Bruno's trial, performing a similar role in the later trial of Galileo. This man wrote numerous papers attacking the oath of allegiance required of English Catholics by James I.
Bruno's teachings also got him excommunicated from a religious group named for this German priest whose 95 Theses kickstarted the Protestant Revolution.

bonuses.leadin: Per a Richard Ellman biography, Samuel Beckett was told to “let it stand” when he included the phrase “Come in” in his transcription of this novel, though it was actually a response to a knock on the door. For 10 points each:
parts: Unverified anecdotes that Beckett supplied to Ellman may have distorted the critical perception of what final novel by James Joyce?

Beckett originated the critical paradigm of conflating Joyce with Stephen Dedalus in an essay whose title names these [emphasize] three Italians. That essay’s title uses periods to indicate the centuries between these three Italians and Joyce.

Beckett’s essay ends by comparing two of these constructs respectively dubbed “conical” and “spherical.” One of these constructs’ “absolute progression” and “guaranteed consummation” is contrasted with the other’s “flux.”
answers: Dante Alighieri AND Giordano Bruno AND Giambattista Vico [accept Dante Alighieri in place of “Dante Alighieri”; accept Iordanus Brunus Nolanus or Filippo Bruno in place of “Giordano Bruno”; accept Giovan Battista Vico in place of “Giambattista Vico”; accept “Dante ... Bruno . Vico .. Joyce”]
Purgatories [accept word-forms; accept Terrestrial Paradises; reject “Paradises”] (The two Purgatories are Dante’s, in The Divine Comedy, and Joyce’s, in Work in Progress.)

packet.name: 07 - Kasiński et al, Marvin et al

answers_sanitized: Dante Alighieri AND Giordano Bruno AND Giambattista Vico [accept Dante Alighieri in place of "Dante Alighieri"; accept Iordanus Brunus Nolanus or Filippo Bruno in place of "Giordano Bruno"; accept Giovan Battista Vico in place of "Giambattista Vico"; accept "Dante ... Bruno . Vico .. Joyce"]
Purgatories [accept word-forms; accept Terrestrial Paradises; reject "Paradises"] (The two Purgatories are Dante's, in The Divine Comedy, and Joyce's, in Work in Progress.)

Beckett's essay ends by comparing two of these constructs respectively dubbed "conical" and "spherical." One of these constructs' "absolute progression" and "guaranteed consummation" is contrasted with the other's "flux."

bonuses.leadin: Barefoot pilgrims prostrate themselves before a barefoot Mary in this artist’s Madonna of Loreto, which is located in the Basilica of Sant’Agostino. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this artist whose few altarpieces include the Madonna of the Rosary. Three men strain to raise a diagonally arranged old man against a dark background in this artist’s Crucifixion of Saint Peter.

Caravaggio and his followers Caracciolo and Jusepe de Ribera were a part of this artistic period that typically used greater contrast in their lighting than the earlier Renaissance and Mannerist styles.

Jusepe de Ribera taught this late-Baroque Neapolitan painter of the Madonna of the Carmelites, who was nicknamed “fa presto” for his productivity. This artist of The Fall of the Rebel Angels and Rubens Painting ‘The Allegory of Peace’ decorated El Escorial.
answers: Caravaggio [or Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio or Michelangelo Merisi or Michele Angelo Merigi]
answers_sanitized: Caravaggio [or Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio or Michelangelo Merisi or Michele Angelo Merigi]

parts_sanitized: Name this artist whose few altarpieces include the Madonna of the Rosary. Three men strain to raise a diagonally arranged old man against a dark background in this artist's Crucifixion of Saint Peter.

Caravaggio and his followers Caracciolo and Jusepe de Ribera were a part of this artistic period that typically used greater contrast in their lighting than the earlier Renaissance and Mannerist styles.

Jusepe de Ribera taught this late-Baroque Neapolitan painter of the Madonna of the Carmelites, who was nicknamed "fa presto" for his productivity. This artist of The Fall of the Rebel Angels and Rubens Painting 'The Allegory of Peace' decorated El Escorial.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the metaphor of a circle with center everywhere and circumference nowhere, for 10 points each.
parts: In his essay “Pascal’s Sphere,” Jorge Luis Borges (“BOR-hess”) supposed that the metaphor began to be applied to the universe after this astronomer formulated a heliocentric model of the Solar System.

Borges identified this author of The Ash Wednesday Supper as the first thinker to describe the universe as having center everywhere and circumference nowhere. This Italian was burned at the stake for heresy in 1600.

Against Borges, Karsten Harries contends that this German cardinal made way for the Copernican Revolution by applying the metaphor to the universe a century before Bruno in his 1440 book On Learned Ignorance.
answers: Nicholas of Cusa [or Nicolaus Cusanus or Nicholas of Kues; prompt on Nicholas or Nicolaus]
answers_sanitized: Nicholas of Cusa [or Nicolaus Cusanus or Nicholas of Kues; prompt on Nicholas or Nicolaus]

Against Borges, Karsten Harries contends that this German cardinal made way for the Copernican Revolution by applying the metaphor to the universe a century before Bruno in his 1440 book On Learned Ignorance.

bonuses.leadin: There is a thought experiment about one of these animals which is exactly halfway between a bale of hay and a bucket of water, and dies of hunger and thirst because it can’t decide which one to go to. For 10 points:
parts: Name this type of animal, the subject of a thought experiment named after Jean Buridan.
Nuccio Ordine wrote an essay examining this philosopher’s thought using the donkey as a metaphor. This Italian was burned at the stake by the Roman Inquisition in 1600.
This husband of G. E. M. Anscombe wrote about the peculiar type of anaphora in sentences like “Every farmer who owns a donkey beats it.” With Frege, he names a problem for expressivism.
answers: Peter Geach NOTE: Do not reveal the alternate answers to the first part if they miss it.
answers_sanitized: Peter Geach NOTE: Do not reveal the alternate answers to the first part if they miss it.

bonuses.leadin: This thinker was the subject of an important monograph by Frances Yates, who argued that his books, such as The Expulsion of the Triumphant Beast, were influenced by Hermetic Tradition. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this philosopher who was executed for espousing a belief in the infinitude of the universe.
Bruno’s views on the infinitude of the universe were inspired by the work of this scientist, who wrote On the Revolution of the Heavenly Spheres and advocated heliocentrism.
Less controversially, Bruno championed this movement, which was initially popularized by Plotinus in his book The Enneads.

bonuses.leadin: The title character of this opera dies after sniffing poisoned flowers sent by her rival, who also loves Maurizio. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this opera whose central conflict stems from a love triangle involving the Princess of Bouillon, the Count of Saxony, and the title actress.

Like Francesco Cilea, the composer of Adriana Lecouvreur, this composer is best known for his repertoire of verismo works, including one that ends with Maddalena going to the guillotine with her lover, the poet Andrea Chénier.
A more famous example of verismo is this Ruggero Leoncavallo opera, in which Canio sings “Vesti la giubba” after discovering that his wife Nedda has been unfaithful.
